name: Length and Frequency of Residential Stays
consequences: Extending individual stays beyond the planned 2–8 week baseline increases cumulative event capture per participant but consumes limited bed-days and reduces total enrollment within the 3-year window. A 10-week cap on unproductive admissions trades bed utilization against the risk of undersampling rarer phenotypes (e.g., sleep-terror clusters occurring in week 8). Monthly throughput, per-participant staffing burden, and the 50–70 participant enrollment target are all directly sensitive to this parameter.
options: Maintain the 2–8 week core stay with optional 2-week extensions capped at 10 weeks total, allowing shorter admissions for high-yield participants to free capacity for additional enrollments and reduce per-bed staff intensity.

Offer 12-week baseline stays for all participants to maximize the probability of capturing at least one event per admission, accepting a 30–40% reduction in total enrollable participant slots within the 3-year period.

Implement a dynamic stay-length protocol where participants with zero events after week 4 receive a brief home-actigraphy interlude and re-admission only if actigraphy flags probable episodes, reducing idle residential occupancy while maintaining event-capture ambition.
review: Longer stays guarantee higher per-person event yields but shrink total sample size and increase staffing complexity during low-yield weeks. None of the options address the tension between rare-event confidence (needing weeks of 24/7 monitoring) and phenotypic diversity (needing diverse participants), which is constrained by facility size, not just individual-stay duration.

name: Recruitment Source and Eligibility Breadth
consequences: Narrowing recruitment to University Hospital Bonn sleep clinic guarantees pre-screened, complex cases but reduces speed and total yield; broadening to include self-referred participants via the DGSFM network and primary-care neurologist channels accelerates enrollment but introduces heterogeneous phenotypes and higher screening burden. Participant quality directly affects the 70–80% event-yield assumption and the credibility of phenotyping findings—if mild cases dominate, event frequencies may fall below prediction, extending timelines.
options: Prioritize recruitment through the University Hospital Bonn sleep clinic and regional tertiary-care neurologists, accepting slower enrollment (estimated 8–12 participants per year in year one) in exchange for rigorous pre-selection and higher event-yield confidence.

Simultaneously activate recruitment through the DGSFM network, patient advocacy groups, and online symptom-screening questionnaires distributed via German sleep medicine forums, targeting 15–20 year-one enrollments with intensive screening to manage phenotypic heterogeneity.

Establish a two-tier intake model: fast-track admission for specialist-referred participants with documented prior events, and a parallel prospective screening cohort of self-referred candidates who undergo 4-week home actigraphy before residential admission, filtering for objective nocturnal movement patterns.
review: Broad recruitment accelerates enrollment but dilutes event yield if mild cases predominate, lengthening stays and reducing statistical power for Aim 2. None of the options quantify the tradeoff between recruitment pace and the actual per-person event frequency achieved in real German sleep clinic populations, which is unmeasured.

name: Sensor Granularity and Tiered Escalation Protocol
consequences: The planned tiered model—low-burden nightly monitoring with enhanced PSG on first, mid, and final nights plus escalation after technician flags—balances naturalistic conditions against clinical-grade resolution. Tightening the escalation threshold (calling in full PSG sooner) increases event characterization fidelity but raises labor and operational costs; relaxing it (fewer escalation nights) preserves budget but risks missing recurrence morphology and confounding triggers. The event-triage algorithm's sensitivity and specificity directly depend on the ground-truth labels produced by this tier structure.
options: Deploy escalation PSG only after confirmed technician flags and dual-rater consensus on nightly low-burden data, reserving clinical-grade montages for ~10–15% of nights per participant and minimizing unnecessary technician calls.

Implement scheduled escalation on every other night (or every third night) regardless of flags, providing denser ground truth for algorithm training but incurring approximately 30–40% higher technician and equipment-utilization costs over 3 years.

Establish a pilot-adaptive escalation policy: during months 1–6 of year one, escalate after every technician flag to generate maximal ground-truth labels; after month 6, reduce escalation frequency based on measured false-alarm rates and algorithm performance, optimizing cost per high-confidence event label.
review: More frequent escalation improves algorithm training but compounds staffing costs and risks technician fatigue, undermining the safety-and-sustainability aim. No option addresses the risk that technician flags may themselves be noisy or biased, rendering escalation decisions suboptimal if based on unvalidated detection rules.

name: Control Group Recruitment and Matching Strategy
consequences: A matched control group is essential for benchmarking event-triage tools and establishing baseline false-positive rates, but recruiting and retaining healthy controls for 2–8 week residential stays is challenging and costly—controls lack the symptom-driven motivation that NREM-parasomnia participants possess. Loose matching (age and sex only) accelerates control enrollment but reduces the statistical power of comparisons; tight matching (age, sex, occupational sleep exposure, caffeine/alcohol habits) strengthens associations but requires intensive screening and recruitment from specific occupational populations (e.g., shift workers), delaying enrollment and potentially inflating control costs.
options: Recruit controls from the general Bonn population aged 18–65 via public advertising, screen for absence of parasomnia and major sleep disorders, and admit them with minimal demographic matching beyond age and sex, aiming for 15–20 controls by month 18.

Implement matched-pair recruitment by occupational stratum (e.g., healthcare shift workers, academics with irregular sleep, factory workers), requiring longer screening but ensuring controls reflect real-world nocturnal movement diversity and yielding higher-fidelity sensor baseline profiles.

Obtain control data opportunistically from healthy research staff or student volunteers enrolled for 3–5 night residential stays concurrent with NREM-parasomnia participant admissions, reducing recruitment burden but introducing volunteer-bias effects and limited sample size (estimated 8–12 controls).
review: Controls strengthen algorithm validation but compete with NREM-parasomnia enrollment for limited residential beds and technician capacity. No option quantifies how many controls are actually needed to detect sensor false-positive rates with statistical precision, leaving the bed-allocation tradeoff unresolved.

name: Data-Sharing Model and Repository Selection
consequences: The plan restricts video to local storage and deposits de-identified physiological data under data use agreements, supporting privacy and ethics compliance but limiting secondary-analysis visibility and reproducibility. Choosing a specialized sleep repository (e.g., the Sleep Heart Health Study archive or a BIDS-compliant EEG repository) ensures discoverability by the sleep neuroscience community but entails additional curation labor; depositing in a general biomedical data repository (e.g., GigaDB, Zenodo) broadens reach but may lack sleep-specific metadata standards, reducing usability. The decision affects replication capacity, citation impact, and the credibility of Aim 3 algorithm benchmarks.
options: Deposit de-identified EEG and sensor time-series in the OpenNeuro platform (EEG-BIDS compliant) with public access and a 3-month embargo, enabling rapid secondary-analysis uptake and facilitating independent algorithm replication while maintaining privacy via de-identification and restricted-release video data.

Submit curated datasets to the Sleep Heart Health Study ancillary data archive and partner with the Sleep Research Society for peer review before public release, ensuring domain-specific validation and conservative governance but requiring 12–18 month publication lead-times to coordinate embargo periods.

Create a controlled-access data portal within the Bonn University research infrastructure, releasing data only to researchers who sign data use agreements affirming non-commercial use and parasomnia-specific research intent, maximizing privacy protection but limiting discoverability and requiring manual access-request processing by the study coordinator.
review: Public deposition accelerates replication but creates privacy risk if de-identification is imperfect; controlled access protects participants but cedes visibility and secondary-analysis value. None of the options address whether the long-term institutional commitment to curating and maintaining the repository exists beyond the 3-year project horizon.

name: Event-Triage Algorithm Development Scope and Validation Rigor
consequences: Aim 3 calls for semi-automated event-triage tools benchmarked against dual human scorer agreement, not autonomous diagnosis. Expanding the model to predict parasomnia *subtype* (sleep terror vs. sleepwalking vs. confusional arousal) and *severity* multiplies ground-truth labeling effort and extends algorithm development into year three; restricting scope to binary detection (event vs. non-event) completes benchmarking faster but forgoes clinically actionable subtyping. The team's single computational neuroscience postdoc and one data engineer must balance development time against Aim 2 phenotyping analysis and manuscript production.
options: Develop a binary event-detection algorithm (parasomnia event vs. normal sleep) validated against dual-rater consensus on the first-night and escalation-night PSG cohort, completing benchmarking by month 30 and allocating remaining postdoc and engineer time to Aim 2 phenotypic analysis and publication.

Implement a three-class classifier (sleep terror, sleepwalking, confusional arousal) using stratified training data from escalation nights, requiring additional annotation and cross-validation but yielding clinically informative subtype probabilities and justifying a phase-two proposal focused on subtype-specific triggers and interventions.

Construct an ensemble approach combining rule-based heuristics (e.g., EEG delta burst detection, EMG threshold crossing) with a lightweight supervised model (logistic regression or random forest) to reduce overfitting risk on the limited ground-truth dataset, sacrificing subtype resolution for generalization robustness and enabling transparent, interpretable predictions for clinical translation.
review: Subtype classification is clinically valuable but strains labeling capacity and risks overfitting on limited ground truth; binary detection is faster and more reliable but reduces the algorithm's clinical utility for participant feedback and follow-up studies. No option addresses how to handle ambiguous or borderline event labels from the dual raters, which may degrade classifier performance more than sample size alone.

name: External Advisory Board Authority and Mid-Course Correction Triggers
consequences: The plan specifies a 3-member external board reviewing progress every 6 months, with the PI holding operational authority and reallocations above €50K requiring approval. Granting the board veto power over participant recruitment criteria, stay-length policies, or sensor protocols strengthens independent oversight and publication credibility but slows decision-making during crises (e.g., safety incidents, recruitment shortfalls); reserving their role as advisory only preserves flexibility but risks groupthink and publication bias. The failure trigger (month 18: <25 participants or <40 adjudicated events) may arrive too late to salvage the project if earlier interventions would have succeeded.
options: Empower the external board with formal approval authority over any changes to inclusion criteria, stay extensions beyond 10 weeks, or escalation protocols, requiring consensus or documented dissent; maintain the month-18 trigger with a supplemental month-12 advisory checkpoint allowing the PI to request early board intervention if enrollment or event yield fall below 50% of plan.

Keep the board in a purely advisory role (recommendations only, no veto), with the PI retaining unilateral authority to modify recruitment, stay length, and sensor policies; replace the rigid month-18 gate with rolling quarterly reports to the board flagging enrollment pace and event-yield trajectory, enabling early warning without formal failure triggers.

Establish a decision-rights matrix: the board has veto authority over changes to safety protocols, informed-consent language, and data-sharing commitments; the PI retains autonomy over recruitment sourcing, stay-length policies, and algorithm-development priorities; introduce a month-12 board review with explicit go/no-go gates for enrollment (≥15 participants) and event capture (≥25 events), with authority to recommend (but not mandate) protocol modifications.
review: Strong board oversight protects scientific integrity but creates governance friction during fast-changing early operations; weak oversight preserves agility but risks unchecked drift and ethical erosion. No option clarifies how the board will assess success if the month-18 trigger is missed but the program is still generating usable data—a judgment call that the board's charter does not yet address.

name: Domestic Environment Authenticity versus Safety Infrastructure Density
consequences: Adding more visible safety equipment (barrier rails, padded walls, motion-triggered lighting) reduces injury risk but triggers self-conscious behavior that dampens naturalistic parasomnia expression—the same suppression effect that makes hospital environments unsuitable for this population. Conversely, minimizing visible safety cues improves event authenticity but raises incident liability and staff response burden.
options: Design safety interventions as subtle architectural elements—low-profile floor beveling, recessed window restrictors, and disguised door alarms integrated into domestic fixtures—so they remain invisible to participants while maintaining clinical protection standards.
Establish separate 'naturalistic' and 'maximal-safety' suite configurations, rotating participants between them after week 2 to capture both baseline authentic behavior and stress-response patterns.

Prioritize safety through continuous remote monitoring with immediate technician response rather than environmental barriers, accepting higher staffing costs and response-time variability to preserve the residential feel.
review: The core tension is that parasomnia expressivity requires genuine domesticity while liability requires visible safeguards; none of the options fully reconciles the participant's perceptual experience with the actual safety density, so subtle design may simply hide risk rather than address the underlying conflict.

name: Participant Compensation Structure and Stay-Duration Incentives
consequences: Flat per-night compensation (€80 + meals) treats all stay lengths equally but provides weak incentive for longer admissions when event capture is slow, risking premature discharge before sufficient episodes accrue. Escalating or bonus compensation for extended stays increases retention but may attract participants motivated by payment rather than symptom resolution, potentially skewing phenotypic representation.
options: Implement a sliding-scale compensation model with higher per-night rates for stays exceeding 4 weeks, plus a completion bonus contingent on reaching a minimum event threshold jointly negotiated with the participant to preserve motivation alignment.

Offer fixed lump-sum compensation indexed to stay duration (€2000 for 2 weeks, €4500 for 4 weeks, €7000 for 6+ weeks) announced upfront so participants can plan financially without in-stay manipulation of their commitment.

Decouple monetary compensation from stay duration and instead provide outcome-based recognition—publication authorship eligibility, priority access to future clinical consultations, or discretionary support for accommodations—to attract intrinsically motivated participants and reduce financial coercion effects.
review: The tension is between sustaining engagement during low-event periods and avoiding adverse selection of participants driven by financial desperation rather than clinical engagement; all three approaches still leave unaddressed the psychological burden of being hospitalized without guaranteed event capture.

name: Equipment Procurement Sequencing and Tiered Capability Ramp
consequences: Purchasing the full sensor suite upfront (dry-electrode EEG headbands, mattress sensors, infrared cameras, PSG-grade scalp EEG, EOG, EMG) incurs €450K capital cost and integration complexity before confirming that facilities, staffing, or participant recruitment can sustain operations. Deferring to a 'learn-by-doing' approach reduces initial expense but risks 6–12 month delays in data collection and algorithm development if core equipment proves incompatible or inadequate.
options: Execute a phased equipment rollout: launch year one with dry-electrode headbands, contact-free mattress sensors, and synchronized microphones only; integrate full PSG and infrared systems in month 9–12 after pilot data confirm signal quality, annotation workflow viability, and participant tolerance thresholds.

Invest in modular, swappable sensor hardware and a vendor-agnostic acquisition middleware from month one, accepting higher upfront engineering cost but enabling rapid sensor substitution if early data reveal systematic noise, drift, or privacy violations without full redeployment.

Partner with a sensor manufacturer for in-kind equipment provision and technical support in exchange for co-authorship on methods papers and early access to de-identified data, shifting capital risk to the vendor while ceding some autonomy over hardware evolution and data retention timelines.
review: The tension is between fiscal prudence (defer spending) and operational momentum (full capability from day one); the modular-middleware option adds complexity and cost that may exceed the savings from deferral if early pilots validate all sensors, wasting resources on flexibility not needed.

name: Inter-Rater Scoring Workflows and Computational Triage Calibration Cadence
consequences: Requiring all-nights dual-human scoring for validation ensures high-confidence AASM labeling but demands approximately 2,000–3,000 independent scoring hours across 50–70 participants, consuming the clinical psychologist's capacity and delaying algorithm development by months. Automating triage early to reduce manual review burden risks embedding systematic bias in the training set that persists through peer review, damaging credibility if downstream clinical deployment reveals false-negative events.
options: Implement 'adaptive sampling': dual-score 100% of enhanced nights (first, mid-stay, final) plus all technician-flagged events, then stratify remaining nights into high-, medium-, and low-confidence tiers based on sensor variance and computational predictions, scoring only high-confidence candidates to cap total labor at 1,000 hours while maintaining control over algorithm bias.

Establish an independent scoring consortium with three regional sleep centers (Munich, Hamburg, Berlin) rotating annotation duties on a formal data-sharing agreement, distributing labor and creating built-in inter-site validation, though requiring ethics approval amendments and 4–6 month negotiation overhead.

Defer all computational triage refinement until month 18, after dual-scoring of the first 30–40 participants is complete, to establish a large labeled dataset with consensus ground truth; trade off early algorithm publication for a more robust training corpus and reduced risk of premature validation claims.
review: The tension is between annotation labor capacity (dual-scoring all nights is unsustainable) and algorithm trustworthiness (early automation risks hidden bias); adaptive sampling reduces labor but introduces selection bias by stratifying what gets validated, and the consortium option trades speed for institutional credibility at unknown governance cost.

name: Matched Control Recruitment and Phenotypic Matching Depth
consequences: Enrolling healthy controls with minimal exclusions (no sleep disorders, no major medications) ensures clean physiological baselines but provides only surface-level comparison if their arousal patterns, micro-movements, or sensor noise profiles differ subtly from parasomnia cohort due to unmeasured factors like age, body composition, or sleep architecture. Tight matching on sleep stage distribution, REM/NREM ratios, or sleep onset latency requires pre-screening polysomnography for controls, doubling recruitment friction and cost.
options: Recruit controls without pre-screening PSG, using only questionnaires and clinical interview to exclude active sleep disorders, then use post-hoc statistical adjustment to control for age, BMI, and self-reported sleep quality, accepting residual heterogeneity but maximizing enrollment efficiency.

Conduct abbreviated screening PSG (two nights in the lab, standard 10–20 electrode montage) for all control candidates to quantify sleep architecture, arousal frequency, and REM/NREM balance before residential admission, ensuring phenotypic matching but adding €500–800 cost and 2–3 month recruitment lag per participant.

Enroll a 'stratified control' design with distinct arms—young healthy (18–35), middle-aged healthy (36–50), older healthy (51–65)—matching age bands to the parasomnia cohort without individual PSG pre-screening, balancing enrollment ease with approximate population-level comparability for sensor false-positive benchmarking.
review: The tension is between recruitment feasibility (no pre-screening) and baseline validity (tight phenotypic matching); both extremes leave unaddressed whether control false positives are genuinely comparable to parasomnia false negatives, since controls may have rare micro-arousals never flagged as events anyway.

name: External Advisory Board Decision Authority and Governance Escalation Pathways
consequences: Requiring board approval above €50K for budget reallocations introduces oversight but also creates delay (6-month review cycles) if urgent equipment substitution, staffing adjustment, or protocol amendment is needed mid-quarter to respond to unexpected facility issues or enrollment shortfalls. Granting the PI unilateral authority accelerates decisions but risks mission drift if the PI reallocates resources toward secondary exploratory aims without scrutiny.
options: Establish a 'tiered escalation' model: the PI approves reallocation up to €25K autonomously for operational contingencies, requires board ratification within 30 days for €25–75K changes (with provisions for emergency verbal approval), and seeks full deliberation above €75K, balancing speed and oversight.

Create a quarterly 'rolling governance' structure in which the advisory board meets 6 weeks before each calendar quarter, receives projected spending and risk scenarios in advance, and pre-authorizes contingency decision ranges (e.g., 'approve equipment substitution up to 15% cost variance if performance metrics remain on track'), reducing mid-quarter bottlenecks.

Delegate financial authority entirely to the PI with mandatory post-hoc disclosure to the board at each review cycle and a 30-day window for the board to file formal objection, forcing transparent reporting while preserving agility, though risking irreversible decisions that the board cannot retroactively reverse.
review: The tension is between agile operations (PI authority) and institutional accountability (board veto); even tiered and rolling structures introduce lag for time-sensitive decisions, and pre-authorized ranges risk scope creep if cost overruns exceed the negotiated bounds.

name: Publication Strategy and Preliminary-Finding Disclosure Timing
consequences: Publishing methods-validation papers early (by month 18–20) builds credibility and secures career advancement for junior team members, but releasing results before full 50–70 participant enrollment risks criticism that event-triage sensitivity/specificity estimates are based on small, potentially unrepresentative cohorts. Waiting until month 32–36 for complete data maximizes statistical power but delays team recognition and allows competing facilities to preempt methodological claims.
options: Adopt a 'rolling publication' cadence: issue a methods protocol paper by month 12 (approved pre-registration via OSF), preliminary longitudinal phenotyping results at month 20 with explicit power-and-limitation disclosure (N=30–35 participants, interim analysis), and final event-triage benchmark paper at month 36 with complete dataset, accepting minor redundancy in exchange for iterative visibility.

Negotiate publication agreements with the pharmaceutical industry partner (if secured) to ensure adequate review period without embargoes; publish first methods paper independently at month 18, then coordinate clinical-translation papers jointly with pharma input, structuring collaboration to protect academic primacy while honoring partner timelines.

Consolidate all findings into 2–3 comprehensive manuscripts submitted simultaneously at month 34–36 to high-impact journals, deferring early disclosure and accepting shorter team recognition windows in exchange for unified, complete stories that are less vulnerable to replication concerns or selective-reporting criticism.
review: The tension is between early visibility (and career momentum) and statistical maturity (larger cohort, lower false-positive risk); rolling publication exposes preliminary findings that may not replicate, while deferral delays team advancement and risks scooping by other labs, neither option fully resolving the publish-pressure versus scientific-rigor tradeoff.

name: Domestic Simulation Depth and Staffing Coverage Coupling
consequences: More furniture, personal-object curation, and circadian-aligned lighting create authentic sleeping conditions that encourage spontaneous episodes, but require additional nighttime staff for safety supervision of less-clinical environments and increase the risk of annotation confusion (technician must distinguish intentional resident behavior from parasomnia). Light staffing forces more austere, monitored spaces that reduce false alarms and administrative burden but suppress the naturalistic triggering context that parasomnia events need.
options: Furnish 6 of 8 suites as fully domestic bedrooms with ambient lighting, personal bedside tables, and closets, scheduling two rotating technicians per night per domestic suite block and one technician covering both remaining clinical-spec suites, then after 12 months assess event yield and staffing safety incidents to decide whether to scale domestic design to remaining suites.

Maintain all 8 suites in a hybrid medical-residential aesthetic with standardized safety-first furniture, standard institutional bedding, and clinical-grade ambient lighting, requiring only one technician per night for the full facility, and use the staff time savings to deepen inter-rater scoring reliability and weekly video annotation meetings.

Randomize suite assignment so that participants experience alternating weeks in a domestic configuration and a clinical configuration in counterbalanced order, enabling within-subject comparison of event frequency and annotation workload burden but requiring staggered staffing that may create coverage gaps during transition weeks.
review: The core tension is that domestic comfort triggers parasomnia expression, yet introduces safety and annotation complexity that pure staffing headcount alone cannot solve. None of the options directly addresses whether overnight technicians receive formal behavioral-sleep training to distinguish parasomnia from normal sleep movement, a prerequisite for accurate real-time annotation regardless of environment aesthetics.

name: Participant Stay Duration Flexibility and Recruitment Targeting
consequences: Shorter fixed stays (e.g., 2–3 weeks) reduce per-participant cost and increase throughput, enabling enrollment of 60+ participants and broader phenotypic diversity, but miss frequent-episode participants who remain unproductive in short windows. Longer adaptive stays (2–10 weeks) capture events from low-frequency participants and allow within-subject longitudinal pattern analysis, but reduce throughput, concentrate budget on fewer participants, and risk recruitment bottlenecks if exclusion during screening is strict.
options: Standardize all admissions to a fixed 3-week stay with a 1-week optional extension only for participants who capture zero events, capping extensions at 10 total participants, and weight recruitment messaging toward participants with reported episodes at least twice per week to maximize event yield within the fixed window.

Adopt a variable-stay protocol (2–10 weeks) stratified on baseline self-reported episode frequency: participants reporting ≥4 episodes per week enter for 2 weeks, those reporting 2–3 per week enter for 4 weeks, and those reporting 2 per month or uncertain frequency enter for up to 10 weeks, with mid-stay assessment gates that allow early discharge if two independent parasomnia events are captured.

Implement a rolling cohort model where participants arrive for a fixed 2-week initial stay, then a subset of the most productive participants (≥2 events captured) are invited to return for a second 2-week stay within 3 months, creating multiple longitudinal observation points per person while keeping per-admission duration short and predictable for staffing and facility scheduling.
review: The tension is between maximizing total events captured (favoring long, variable stays) and maximizing recruitment diversity and operational predictability (favoring short, fixed stays). None of the options explicitly estimates the breakeven point—how many additional events per long-stay participant justify the reduction in throughput and phenotypic diversity, or vice versa.

name: Matched Control Enrollment Concurrency and Phenotypic Stringency
consequences: Recruiting matched controls in parallel with case participants ensures equivalent facility conditions and sensor exposure for the triage-algorithm benchmarking, but diverts recruitment effort and bed capacity from the primary NREM parasomnia cohort, potentially delaying event-capture milestones. Sequential control enrollment (after case recruitment reaches 50% completion) preserves case throughput but introduces temporal confounds (e.g., sensor firmware updates, seasonality, staff experience drift between case and control periods).
options: Enroll matched controls concurrently with cases at a 1:1 ratio, prioritizing age, sex, and baseline actigraphy-measured sleep efficiency as matching criteria, and staff the facility for 16 concurrent occupants (8 cases rotating plus 8 controls), accepting a need for a temporary second residential property lease or a 4-month facility expansion delay to accommodate capacity.

Front-load the case cohort (target 50–55 NREM parasomnia participants over months 1–24), then recruit 20–25 matched controls in months 22–36 using historical demographic and actigraphy data from the case cohort to define matching strata, reducing concurrent bed pressure but accepting that control baseline sensors will not be identical to case sensors if hardware refreshes or software patches occur between cohorts.

Use unmatched healthy sleepers from the same geographic region (recruited through open community advertising) as ad hoc controls on a rolling basis, assigning controls to overnight facility stays on weeks when case bed occupancy is below 6 suites, minimizing recruitment targeting effort but accepting looser phenotypic matching and potential seasonal bias if winter admissions cluster differently between groups.
review: The core tension is temporal homogeneity (concurrent control enrollment) versus operational efficiency (sequential). None of the options addresses how to validate that "matched" controls are truly representative of normal parasomnia-free sleep, or whether the triage algorithm requires truly matched controls or can generalize from a larger, less-matched healthy-sleep comparison cohort.

name: Inter-Rater Scoring Workflow and Annotation-Bottleneck Resolution
consequences: Full dual-rater scoring of all recorded nights (8 suites × 2–10 weeks × 365 nights potential = thousands of hours of video review annually) guarantees interrater reliability estimates for publication but creates a staffing and cost bottleneck that can slow publication timelines and consume contingency budget. Single-rater annotation with periodic spot-check audits (e.g., 20% of nights re-scored by a second rater) reduces workload but weakens confidence in disagreement rates and may miss systematic scorer drift.
options: Establish a dedicated external scoring consortium: contract two independent sleep-medicine trained raters at an academic center 2 hours away to score all nights blind to participant identity and condition (case vs. control), compensating them at €40 per night-review hour, and schedule their reviews on a 6-week delay to allow real-time technician annotation to inform their sampling strategy.

Implement a tiered scoring protocol where the nighttime technician provides real-time preliminary scoring for all nights, the first independent rater then scores 100% of nights flagged as event-positive or borderline by the technician, and the second independent rater scores 100% of event-positive nights plus a random 15% sample of flagged and non-flagged nights, reducing the total dual-scoring burden by approximately 60% while preserving specificity estimates.

Leverage automated activity-detection algorithms (e.g., pose estimation from video keyframes, accelerometer-derived movement indices from mattress sensors) to pre-filter candidate event windows, then restrict human dual-rater scoring to the top-ranked 500 candidate events per year per participant, freeing technician and rater time for deeper annotation of confirmed events and allowing faster algorithm iteration.
review: The tension is between publishable interrater reliability metrics (demanding 100% dual scoring) and sustainable staffing (favoring sampling and automation). None of the options directly addresses how to prevent rater fatigue or unconscious drift in scoring criteria over a 36-month annotation period, or whether training and recalibration checkpoints should be built into any scoring workflow.

name: Event-Triage Algorithm Development Staging and Validation Targets
consequences: Rapid algorithm prototyping (starting in month 6 with preliminary event-flagging rules) enables early real-time technician support and allows months for iterative refinement, but risks publishing or deploying an undertested model if phase-one data is sparse or unrepresentative. Deferring algorithm development until month 24 (after 50–70 participants are enrolled and case series characteristics are well-understood) ensures robust training data but delays real-time decision support and compresses the development timeline into the final grant year.
options: Begin algorithm development in month 6 using the first 15–20 case participants' scored event data to train simple heuristics (e.g., movement magnitude + heart-rate deviation thresholds), test them on held-out months from the same participants, publish preliminary sensitivity/specificity estimates at 24 months, and then refine using the full 50–70 participant cohort in year three with no claim of final optimization.

Defer algorithm development entirely until month 22–24, when full case enrollment is near completion and dual-rater scoring is substantially complete, then train on the entire dataset, use cross-validation and temporal hold-out cohorts, and publish the algorithm performance alongside the final phenotyping analysis at month 36, trading earlier decision support for stronger validation rigor.

Establish a minimal real-time screening rule in month 4 (e.g., alert technician if mattress-sensor movement exceeds 95th percentile during sleep window or acoustic signature matches preprocessed parasomniac sounds), then use technician-flagged events as training labels for a secondary machine-learning model developed from month 12–24, creating two publication tracks: a methods paper on the real-time screening rule and a later algorithm-validation paper on the refined classifier.
review: The tension is between early real-time decision support (favoring rapid prototyping) and robust, publishable validation (favoring deferred, full-data development). None of the options specifies the minimum event count or phenotypic diversity required before algorithm retraining becomes statistically justified, or how to prevent the real-time screening rule from introducing annotation bias if technicians unconsciously over-report events matching the alert signature.

name: Funding Dependency Structure and Pharmaceutical Partnership Terms
consequences: Declining pharmaceutical partnership offers preserves full publication independence and protects against industry priority conflicts, but relies entirely on DFG and University funding, creating vulnerability to German research budget fluctuations and forcing stricter spending discipline. Accepting pharmaceutical partnership funding accelerates budget headroom (enabling larger control cohort or earlier facility expansion) and funds phase-two planning, but triggers 30-day pre-publication review windows and embeds expectation of future collaboration that constrains research directions.
options: Commit to pure public-sector funding (DFG + University Bonn), implement zero-based budgeting with monthly financial reviews to ensure cash flow matches enrollment pace, and explicitly state in the grant proposal that pharmaceutical partnerships are declining to preserve publication timing and research agenda independence.

Negotiate a pharmaceutical partnership agreement that provides €500K additional year-one funding in exchange for a 30-day pre-publication review window and a named acknowledgment clause, but explicitly reserve in writing that all publication decisions, timelines, and content are determined unilaterally by the academic team, and require board approval for any scope expansion that the pharma partner requests.

Structure a research data-licensing arrangement where the pharma partner funds 25% of data-storage and computational costs in return for non-exclusive 2-year access to de-identified physiological data for their own algorithm validation, but explicitly prohibit any joint authorship or early-access claims, isolating the partnership to data-use terms rather than publication control.
review: The core tension is financial security versus editorial independence. None of the options directly addresses reputational risk if the pharma partner's own algorithm or product based on the data later faces regulatory or clinical criticism, or how to manage conflict-of-interest disclosure if the team publishes negative findings about the pharma partner's hypothesis.

name: External Advisory Board Authority and Failure-Trigger Accountability Mechanisms
consequences: A 3-member board with approval authority over budget reallocations (>€50K) and semi-annual review gates ensures external scrutiny and credibility with funders, but concentrates too much gate-keeping power in a small group and can slow operational pivots if the board prioritizes caution over pragmatism. A purely advisory board (no approval authority) preserves PI autonomy and operational agility, but weakens accountability to funders and risks mission drift if the PI's judgment falters without external correction.
options: Establish a formal 5-member advisory board (including two international parasomnia experts, one biostatistician, one regulatory consultant, and one patient advocate) that meets quarterly and has binding veto authority only on scope expansions (e.g., adding RBD as a separate protocol, changes to exclusion criteria), non-binding recommendations on budget reallocations over €75K, and mandatory interim reviews at months 12 and 24 with go/no-go decision thresholds (e.g., ≥25 enrolled participants and ≥40 adjudicated events by month 18 required to continue).

Keep a 3-member advisory board in a purely advisory capacity with no approval authority, but implement a structured governance checklist where the PI must formally document and justify to the board any decision affecting recruitment targets, stay durations, sensor configurations, or publications, with the board recording dissenting opinions in a sealed report to the DFG program officer if consensus fails.

Divide oversight between a 2-member scientific advisory board (sleep medicine experts) with binding veto authority on methods changes, and a separate 2-member operational audit committee (finance + compliance officer) with quarterly budget and safety-incident review authority, separating scientific judgment from fiduciary accountability but risking coordination gaps between the two boards.
review: The tension is between accountability (favoring more authority) and operational agility (favoring pure advisory status). None of the options defines what happens if the advisory board recommends termination at month 18 but the PI disagrees, or how to resolve deadlock without escalating to the funding agency and triggering external investigation.

name: De-Identification Protocol and Public Data Repository Selection
consequences: Strict de-identification (removing all bedroom video, retaining only numeric sensor time-series and scored event labels) minimizes privacy risk and enables rapid public deposition in established repositories (e.g., OpenNeuro, PhysioNet), maximizing scientific impact and long-term data citability. Retaining pseudonymized video or limiting data sharing to restricted-access institutional repositories preserves richer phenotypic information for future collaborators but fragments data ecosystem, reduces citation pressure, and delays third-party reuse.
options: Strip all video data immediately after technician and rater annotation; retain only time-synchronized EEG, accelerometer, heart rate, respiration, and audio-derived (speech/movement amplitude, no content) sensor streams in BIDS format; deposit the complete de-identified dataset in OpenNeuro with a 6-month embargo to allow publication priority, then enable unrestricted download; include a detailed data dictionary documenting preprocessing steps and rater-agreement statistics.

Retain pseudonymized video in a University Hospital Bonn secured data repository with restricted access (single sign-on, institutional affiliation required, executed data use agreements), deposit only physiological time-series in public repositories, and create a two-tier data model where external researchers can access sensor data freely but must apply to the facility director for video access with specified research justification and ethics approval.

Generate synthetic event-window videos (computer-generated avatars performing the postures and movements annotated from real events, seeded with actual accelerometer patterns) to replace raw video, deposit these synthetic vignettes alongside real sensor data in a public repository, and publish a separate methodological validation comparing synthetic-video inter-rater agreement to real-video agreement, trading raw phenotypic fidelity for plausible privacy assurance.
review: The core tension is privacy protection (favoring strict de-identification) versus scientific reuse potential (favoring maximal data richness). None of the options accounts for the possibility that sophisticated individuals or institutions could re-identify participants from time-series patterns alone, or whether de-identified data should include event timestamps that could reveal circadian phenotypes useful for commercial targeting.

name: Phase-Two Proposal Positioning and Longitudinal-Study Expansion Pathway
consequences: Publishing a narrowly scoped phase-one methods paper (demonstrating facility validation and event-capture feasibility) leaves the logical next step ambiguous and invites competitors to propose their own expansion studies. A phase-one paper coupled with a prewritten phase-two grant proposal (submitted before phase-one results are published) reserves intellectual priority and ensures funding continuity if phase-two is approved, but risks commitment to a predetermined direction if phase-one data reveal unexpected findings that would argue for a different expansion focus.
options: Publish the phase-one methods and phenotyping results (Aims 1–2) separately from the algorithm-validation paper (Aim 3), then 6 months later submit a phase-two proposal for a multi-site network expansion (adding a second facility in Munich or Berlin) with harmonized protocols and a pre-registered intervention trial targeting medication-resistant parasomnia, explicitly citing the phase-one publications as proof of feasibility.

Prepare a draft phase-two proposal by month 30 (before final phase-one data analysis is locked) that outlines three contingent expansion pathways (pathway A: if event-capture yield is >80%, propose a 12-month longitudinal re-admission protocol tracking long-term phenotype stability; pathway B: if yield is 50–80%, propose a multi-site replication design; pathway C: if yield is <50%, propose protocol refinement and expanded recruitment in year 3), then select the pathway based on month-24 interim data and submit the finalized proposal at month 32.

Frame the phase-one papers as foundational infrastructure-science contributions (e.g., in *Sleep*, *Nature Neuroscience*, *Journal of Neuroscience Methods*) and defer all phase-two planning until the core publications are accepted, minimizing commitment bias but accepting 6–12 month lag before phase-two funding discussions begin and risking that another group publishes a competitive phase-two study design first.
review: The tension is between intellectual commitment (favoring early phase-two drafting) and methodological flexibility (favoring deferred planning until phase-one data mature). None of the options addresses whether the DFG or pharmaceutical partners would view early phase-two submission positively as strategic planning or negatively as overcommitment before phase-one is complete.

name: RBD Subprotocol Integration and Neurologic Screening Sequencing
consequences: Launching RBD recruitment immediately (month 1) alongside NREM parasomnia cases creates a separate annotation and staffing workstream, requires dedicated neurologic screening expertise, and may dilute NREM event-capture focus if RBD participants are enrolled at high volume. Deferring RBD recruitment until month 12–18 (after NREM protocols are operationally stable) reduces concurrent complexity and allows repurposing of any under-utilized NREM suites, but delays RBD data collection and risks losing interested referrals if the research team is not actively screening.
options: Initiate RBD subprotocol and recruitment in month 3 with a dedicated neurologist co-PI leading screening and a reserved 2-suite RBD-only block, requiring separate budget allocation (approximately €300K additional over 3 years for neurologist time and RBD-specific EMG calibration), separate dual-rater scoring workflows, and a targeted enrollment goal of 15–20 RBD participants by month 36, accepting the management overhead but ensuring phase-one RBD data are available for phase-two multi-site planning.

Maintain NREM-parasomnia focus through month 18, monitor NREM recruitment pace and event-yield achievement against the month-18 go/no-go gate (≥25 participants, ≥40 events), then conditionally launch RBD recruitment in month 19–24 only if NREM metrics are met, using existing suites and rater infrastructure without budget reallocation, reducing setup complexity but accepting that RBD enrollment will be compressed and phase-one RBD N may be too small for robust methods publication.

Establish a single unified overnight-monitoring protocol that enrolls both NREM parasomnia and RBD participants in the same 8-suite facility with harmonized sensor configurations and shared dual-rater scoring workflows, but sub-stratify the neurologic screening and data analysis (RBD cases receive additional sleep-stage-specific EMG interpretation per AASM RBD criteria), creating operational simplicity at the cost of less RBD-specific phenotyping depth in year one and potentially confounding NREM and RBD annotation if raters do not maintain subgroup coding discipline.
review: The core tension is integrated operational efficiency (favoring unified protocol) versus specialized phenotyping depth and recruitment agility (favoring separate RBD launch). None of the options justifies the assumption that RBD neurologic screening can be reliably performed by a sleep-trained physician versus requiring a dedicated sleep neurologist, or estimates how many referred RBD candidates would fail exclusion criteria related to epilepsy or other neurologic comorbidities.
